key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr/Ni FCC-BCC interface with nanoscale Ni segregation enables plastic co-deformation by promoting dislocation activity, twinning, and interfacial shear in an ultrafine eutectic.
engineering_relevance: Designing interface spacing and chemistry in ultrafine eutectics can raise strength while preserving ductility, offering a route to damage-resistant structural alloys for additive manufacturing.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion to create a lamellar Cr-rich BCC / Ni-rich FCC eutectic
Atomic-resolution STEM / HR-STEM to resolve interface structure, orientation relationship, and local chemistry
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension plus correlative TEM to track deformation mechanisms
evidence_signal: In situ mechanical tests showed high flow stresses with large plastic strains and no cracking, while microscopy linked the response to dislocation accumulation, twinning, interfacial shear, and later BCC dislocation mobility.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated in a specific ultrafine eutectic geometry, so translation to other compositions or larger defect populations may depend strongly on controlling interface structure and spacing.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
